Made with MyST

Boundary Control and Parameter Estimation

University of Pisa

Overview¶

The previous lectures focused mostly on distributed controls, where the control acts as a volume source term. This lecture moves to two situations where the same optimal-control structure remains visible, but the functional analytic details become more delicate:

  • controls acting on the boundary;

  • unknown coefficients or material parameters.

The main new point is that the control-to-state map no longer has the same regularity as in the distributed case. Boundary controls interact with trace operators, while parameter controls enter the differential operator itself. Both effects change the correct space in which gradients and optimality conditions should be interpreted.

The logical path is:

  • formulate Neumann boundary control in weak form;

  • prove well-posedness by the trace theorem and Lax-Milgram;

  • derive the adjoint equation and the reduced gradient using a Lagrangian;

  • discuss Dirichlet boundary control through liftings;

  • formulate coefficient identification as a PDE-constrained inverse problem;

  • prove the sensitivity equation for the coefficient-to-state map;

  • derive the adjoint gradient for parameter estimation from a Lagrangian;

  • interpret box constraints as variational inequalities and projection formulas when enough regularity is available.

Throughout the lecture, let Ω⊂Rd\Omega\subset\mathbb R^dΩ⊂Rd be a bounded Lipschitz domain, and let Γ:=∂Ω\Gamma:=\partial\OmegaΓ:=∂Ω.

Boundary Controls and Trace Spaces¶

For a distributed control, the state equation typically has the form

Ay=Bu+fin Ω,\mathcal A y = Bu+f \qquad\text{in }\Omega,Ay=Bu+fin Ω,
(1)

where BBB maps the control space into a volume dual space such as H−1(Ω)H^{-1}(\Omega)H−1(Ω).

For a boundary control, the action of uuu is instead mediated by the trace operator. The fundamental mapping is

γ:H1(Ω)→H1/2(Γ),\gamma:H^1(\Omega)\to H^{1/2}(\Gamma),γ:H1(Ω)→H1/2(Γ),
(2)

which is continuous. Since H1/2(Γ)H^{1/2}(\Gamma)H1/2(Γ) embeds continuously into L2(Γ)L^2(\Gamma)L2(Γ) on bounded Lipschitz boundaries, there is a constant Ctr>0C_{\mathrm{tr}}>0Ctr​>0 such that

∥γv∥L2(Γ)≤Ctr∥v∥H1(Ω)∀v∈H1(Ω).\|\gamma v\|_{L^2(\Gamma)} \le C_{\mathrm{tr}}\|v\|_{H^1(\Omega)} \qquad \forall v\in H^1(\Omega).∥γv∥L2(Γ)​≤Ctr​∥v∥H1(Ω)​∀v∈H1(Ω).
(3)

This estimate is the key reason why Neumann data in L2(Γ)L^2(\Gamma)L2(Γ) define a bounded linear functional on H1(Ω)H^1(\Omega)H1(Ω):

∣∫Γu γv ds∣≤∥u∥L2(Γ)∥γv∥L2(Γ)≤Ctr∥u∥L2(Γ)∥v∥H1(Ω).\left|\int_\Gamma u\,\gamma v\,ds\right| \le \|u\|_{L^2(\Gamma)}\|\gamma v\|_{L^2(\Gamma)} \le C_{\mathrm{tr}}\|u\|_{L^2(\Gamma)}\|v\|_{H^1(\Omega)}.∣∣​∫Γ​uγvds∣∣​≤∥u∥L2(Γ)​∥γv∥L2(Γ)​≤Ctr​∥u∥L2(Γ)​∥v∥H1(Ω)​.
(4)

Thus Neumann boundary control is naturally compatible with weak formulations. Dirichlet boundary control is subtler, because prescribing y=uy=uy=u on Γ\GammaΓ requires uuu to be a trace of an H1(Ω)H^1(\Omega)H1(Ω) function, hence

u∈H1/2(Γ)u\in H^{1/2}(\Gamma)u∈H1/2(Γ)
(5)

is the natural energy space.

A Coercive Neumann Boundary Control Model¶

To avoid the compatibility condition of the pure Neumann Laplacian, we first consider the coercive elliptic equation

{−Δy+σy=fin Ω,∂ny=uon Γ,\begin{cases} -\Delta y+\sigma y=f & \text{in }\Omega,\\ \partial_n y=u & \text{on }\Gamma, \end{cases}{−Δy+σy=f∂n​y=u​in Ω,on Γ,​
(6)

where σ>0\sigma>0σ>0. The pure Neumann case σ=0\sigma=0σ=0 is discussed below.

Let

V:=H1(Ω),U:=L2(Γ),V:=H^1(\Omega), \qquad U:=L^2(\Gamma),V:=H1(Ω),U:=L2(Γ),
(7)

and define

a(y,v):=∫Ω∇y⋅∇v dx+σ∫Ωyv dx,a(y,v):= \int_\Omega \nabla y\cdot\nabla v\,dx + \sigma\int_\Omega yv\,dx,a(y,v):=∫Ω​∇y⋅∇vdx+σ∫Ω​yvdx,
(8)
F(v):=∫Ωfv dx,B(u,v):=∫Γu γv ds.F(v):=\int_\Omega f v\,dx, \qquad B(u,v):=\int_\Gamma u\,\gamma v\,ds.F(v):=∫Ω​fvdx,B(u,v):=∫Γ​uγvds.
(9)

The weak state equation is:

find y∈Vy\in Vy∈V such that

a(y,v)=F(v)+B(u,v)∀v∈V.a(y,v)=F(v)+B(u,v) \qquad \forall v\in V.a(y,v)=F(v)+B(u,v)∀v∈V.
(10)

For a desired state yd∈L2(Ω)y_d\in L^2(\Omega)yd​∈L2(Ω) and a regularization parameter α>0\alpha>0α>0, the boundary control problem is

min⁡u∈UadJ(y,u):=12∥y−yd∥L2(Ω)2+α2∥u∥L2(Γ)2\min_{u\in U_{\mathrm{ad}}} J(y,u) := \frac12\|y-y_d\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 + \frac\alpha2\|u\|_{L^2(\Gamma)}^2u∈Uad​min​J(y,u):=21​∥y−yd​∥L2(Ω)2​+2α​∥u∥L2(Γ)2​
(11)

subject to the weak state equation.

The admissible set is assumed to be nonempty, closed, and convex in L2(Γ)L^2(\Gamma)L2(Γ). A standard example is the box-constrained set

Uad:={u∈L2(Γ):ua≤u≤ub a.e. on Γ},U_{\mathrm{ad}} := \{u\in L^2(\Gamma): u_a\le u\le u_b \text{ a.e. on }\Gamma\},Uad​:={u∈L2(Γ):ua​≤u≤ub​ a.e. on Γ},
(12)

where ua,ub∈L∞(Γ)u_a,u_b\in L^\infty(\Gamma)ua​,ub​∈L∞(Γ) and ua≤ubu_a\le u_bua​≤ub​ a.e.

Well-Posedness of the Neumann State Equation¶

The bilinear form aaa is continuous on V×VV\times VV×V because

∣a(y,v)∣≤∥∇y∥L2(Ω)∥∇v∥L2(Ω)+σ∥y∥L2(Ω)∥v∥L2(Ω)≤C∥y∥H1(Ω)∥v∥H1(Ω).|a(y,v)| \le \|\nabla y\|_{L^2(\Omega)}\|\nabla v\|_{L^2(\Omega)} + \sigma\|y\|_{L^2(\Omega)}\|v\|_{L^2(\Omega)} \le C\|y\|_{H^1(\Omega)}\|v\|_{H^1(\Omega)}.∣a(y,v)∣≤∥∇y∥L2(Ω)​∥∇v∥L2(Ω)​+σ∥y∥L2(Ω)​∥v∥L2(Ω)​≤C∥y∥H1(Ω)​∥v∥H1(Ω)​.
(13)

It is also coercive on H1(Ω)H^1(\Omega)H1(Ω):

a(v,v)=∥∇v∥L2(Ω)2+σ∥v∥L2(Ω)2≥min⁡{1,σ}∥v∥H1(Ω)2.a(v,v) = \|\nabla v\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 + \sigma\|v\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 \ge \min\{1,\sigma\}\|v\|_{H^1(\Omega)}^2.a(v,v)=∥∇v∥L2(Ω)2​+σ∥v∥L2(Ω)2​≥min{1,σ}∥v∥H1(Ω)2​.
(14)

The right-hand side is bounded on VVV. Indeed,

∣F(v)∣≤∥f∥L2(Ω)∥v∥L2(Ω)≤∥f∥L2(Ω)∥v∥H1(Ω),|F(v)| \le \|f\|_{L^2(\Omega)}\|v\|_{L^2(\Omega)} \le \|f\|_{L^2(\Omega)}\|v\|_{H^1(\Omega)},∣F(v)∣≤∥f∥L2(Ω)​∥v∥L2(Ω)​≤∥f∥L2(Ω)​∥v∥H1(Ω)​,
(15)

and the trace estimate gives

∣B(u,v)∣≤Ctr∥u∥L2(Γ)∥v∥H1(Ω).|B(u,v)| \le C_{\mathrm{tr}}\|u\|_{L^2(\Gamma)}\|v\|_{H^1(\Omega)}.∣B(u,v)∣≤Ctr​∥u∥L2(Γ)​∥v∥H1(Ω)​.
(16)

By Lax-Milgram, for every u∈L2(Γ)u\in L^2(\Gamma)u∈L2(Γ) there exists a unique state y(u)∈H1(Ω)y(u)\in H^1(\Omega)y(u)∈H1(Ω) and

∥y(u)∥H1(Ω)≤C(∥f∥L2(Ω)+∥u∥L2(Γ)).\|y(u)\|_{H^1(\Omega)} \le C\left( \|f\|_{L^2(\Omega)} + \|u\|_{L^2(\Gamma)} \right).∥y(u)∥H1(Ω)​≤C(∥f∥L2(Ω)​+∥u∥L2(Γ)​).
(17)

Moreover, the control-to-state map

S:L2(Γ)→H1(Ω),S(u)=y(u),S:L^2(\Gamma)\to H^1(\Omega), \qquad S(u)=y(u),S:L2(Γ)→H1(Ω),S(u)=y(u),
(18)

is affine and continuous. If fff is fixed, its derivative is the linear map S′(u)h=zS'(u)h=zS′(u)h=z, where z∈H1(Ω)z\in H^1(\Omega)z∈H1(Ω) solves

a(z,v)=B(h,v)∀v∈H1(Ω).a(z,v)=B(h,v) \qquad \forall v\in H^1(\Omega).a(z,v)=B(h,v)∀v∈H1(Ω).
(19)

For the pure Neumann equation σ=0\sigma=0σ=0, constants belong to the kernel of the operator. One either works in the quotient space H1(Ω)/RH^1(\Omega)/\mathbb RH1(Ω)/R, or fixes the representative by imposing zero mean:

V0:={v∈H1(Ω):∫Ωv dx=0}.V_0:= \left\{ v\in H^1(\Omega): \int_\Omega v\,dx=0 \right\}.V0​:={v∈H1(Ω):∫Ω​vdx=0}.
(20)

On V0V_0V0​, Poincare’s inequality gives coercivity of a(v,v)=∥∇v∥L2(Ω)2a(v,v)=\|\nabla v\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2a(v,v)=∥∇v∥L2(Ω)2​. If the equation is required to hold for all test functions in H1(Ω)H^1(\Omega)H1(Ω), the data must also satisfy the compatibility condition

∫Ωf dx+∫Γu ds=0.\int_\Omega f\,dx+\int_\Gamma u\,ds=0.∫Ω​fdx+∫Γ​uds=0.
(21)

This is why adding a positive reaction term is often the cleaner model for unconstrained Neumann boundary control.

Reduced Functional and Existence of an Optimal Control¶

Since the state is uniquely determined by uuu, define the reduced functional

j(u):=J(S(u),u).j(u):=J(S(u),u).j(u):=J(S(u),u).
(22)

The estimate above implies that jjj is continuous on L2(Γ)L^2(\Gamma)L2(Γ). The term α2∥u∥L2(Γ)2\frac\alpha2\|u\|_{L^2(\Gamma)}^22α​∥u∥L2(Γ)2​ makes jjj coercive:

j(u)→+∞as∥u∥L2(Γ)→+∞.j(u)\to+\infty \qquad\text{as}\qquad \|u\|_{L^2(\Gamma)}\to+\infty.j(u)→+∞as∥u∥L2(Γ)​→+∞.
(23)

Because SSS is affine and continuous, the tracking term 12∥S(u)−yd∥L2(Ω)2\frac12\|S(u)-y_d\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^221​∥S(u)−yd​∥L2(Ω)2​ is weakly lower semicontinuous. The regularization term is weakly lower semicontinuous as well. Therefore, by the direct method of the calculus of variations, jjj attains a minimizer on every nonempty closed convex set Uad⊂L2(Γ)U_{\mathrm{ad}}\subset L^2(\Gamma)Uad​⊂L2(Γ).

Since α>0\alpha>0α>0, the reduced functional is strictly convex. Hence the optimal control is unique.

Adjoint Equation for Neumann Boundary Control¶

We derive the adjoint through the Lagrangian formalism. The weak state equation is the constraint

a(y,v)−F(v)−B(u,v)=0∀v∈V.a(y,v)-F(v)-B(u,v)=0 \qquad \forall v\in V.a(y,v)−F(v)−B(u,v)=0∀v∈V.
(24)

Introduce the Lagrangian

L(y,u,p)=12∥y−yd∥L2(Ω)2+α2∥u∥L2(Γ)2−[a(y,p)−F(p)−B(u,p)],\mathcal L(y,u,p) = \frac12\|y-y_d\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 + \frac\alpha2\|u\|_{L^2(\Gamma)}^2 - \bigl[ a(y,p)-F(p)-B(u,p) \bigr],L(y,u,p)=21​∥y−yd​∥L2(Ω)2​+2α​∥u∥L2(Γ)2​−[a(y,p)−F(p)−B(u,p)],
(25)

where p∈Vp\in Vp∈V is the Lagrange multiplier. The sign in front of the constraint is a convention; this choice gives the gradient αu+γp\alpha u+\gamma pαu+γp.

Stationarity with respect to ppp gives the state equation:

DpL(y,u,p) ψ=−[a(y,ψ)−F(ψ)−B(u,ψ)]=0∀ψ∈V.D_p\mathcal L(y,u,p)\,\psi = -\bigl[ a(y,\psi)-F(\psi)-B(u,\psi) \bigr] =0 \qquad \forall \psi\in V.Dp​L(y,u,p)ψ=−[a(y,ψ)−F(ψ)−B(u,ψ)]=0∀ψ∈V.
(26)

Stationarity with respect to yyy gives the adjoint equation. For every state variation η∈V\eta\in Vη∈V,

DyL(y,u,p) η=(y−yd,η)L2(Ω)−a(η,p).D_y\mathcal L(y,u,p)\,\eta = (y-y_d,\eta)_{L^2(\Omega)} - a(\eta,p).Dy​L(y,u,p)η=(y−yd​,η)L2(Ω)​−a(η,p).
(27)

Thus

a(η,p)=(y−yd,η)L2(Ω)∀η∈V.a(\eta,p)=(y-y_d,\eta)_{L^2(\Omega)} \qquad \forall \eta\in V.a(η,p)=(y−yd​,η)L2(Ω)​∀η∈V.
(28)

The adjoint problem is well posed by the same Lax-Milgram argument. If the solution is smooth, this weak equation corresponds to

{−Δp+σp=y−ydin Ω,∂np=0on Γ.\begin{cases} -\Delta p+\sigma p=y-y_d & \text{in }\Omega,\\ \partial_n p=0 & \text{on }\Gamma. \end{cases}{−Δp+σp=y−yd​∂n​p=0​in Ω,on Γ.​
(29)

Finally, stationarity with respect to the control gives the derivative in the control direction. For h∈L2(Γ)h\in L^2(\Gamma)h∈L2(Γ),

DuL(y,u,p) h=α(u,h)L2(Γ)+B(h,p)=∫Γ(αu+γp)h ds.D_u\mathcal L(y,u,p)\,h = \alpha(u,h)_{L^2(\Gamma)} + B(h,p) = \int_\Gamma \left( \alpha u+\gamma p \right)h\,ds.Du​L(y,u,p)h=α(u,h)L2(Γ)​+B(h,p)=∫Γ​(αu+γp)hds.
(30)

Because the state and adjoint stationarity equations cancel all implicit state variations, this is the derivative of the reduced functional:

j′(u)h=∫Γ(αu+γp)h ds.j'(u)h = \int_\Gamma \left( \alpha u+\gamma p \right)h\,ds.j′(u)h=∫Γ​(αu+γp)hds.
(31)

Thus the L2(Γ)L^2(\Gamma)L2(Γ)-gradient of the reduced functional is

∇j(u)=αu+γp.\nabla j(u)=\alpha u+\gamma p.∇j(u)=αu+γp.
(32)

If one puts the opposite sign in front of the constraint in the Lagrangian, the adjoint variable changes sign and the same optimality system can be written with αu−γp\alpha u-\gamma pαu−γp instead.

Variational Inequality and Projection Formula¶

Let uˉ∈Uad\bar u\in U_{\mathrm{ad}}uˉ∈Uad​ be the optimal control, with state yˉ\bar yyˉ​ and adjoint pˉ\bar ppˉ​. Since UadU_{\mathrm{ad}}Uad​ is closed and convex, the first-order necessary and sufficient condition is

j′(uˉ)(v−uˉ)≥0∀v∈Uad.j'(\bar u)(v-\bar u)\ge0 \qquad \forall v\in U_{\mathrm{ad}}.j′(uˉ)(v−uˉ)≥0∀v∈Uad​.
(33)

Using the gradient formula, this becomes

(αuˉ+γpˉ,v−uˉ)L2(Γ)≥0∀v∈Uad.(\alpha\bar u+\gamma\bar p,v-\bar u)_{L^2(\Gamma)} \ge0 \qquad \forall v\in U_{\mathrm{ad}}.(αuˉ+γpˉ​,v−uˉ)L2(Γ)​≥0∀v∈Uad​.
(34)

For box constraints, the variational inequality is equivalent to the pointwise projection formula

uˉ=P[ua,ub](−1αγpˉ)a.e. on Γ.\bar u = P_{[u_a,u_b]} \left( -\frac1\alpha\gamma\bar p \right) \qquad\text{a.e. on }\Gamma.uˉ=P[ua​,ub​]​(−α1​γpˉ​)a.e. on Γ.
(35)

Indeed, for almost every boundary point x∈Γx\in\Gammax∈Γ, the scalar condition is

(αuˉ(x)+γpˉ(x))(v−uˉ(x))≥0∀v∈[ua(x),ub(x)].(\alpha\bar u(x)+\gamma\bar p(x))(v-\bar u(x))\ge0 \qquad \forall v\in [u_a(x),u_b(x)].(αuˉ(x)+γpˉ​(x))(v−uˉ(x))≥0∀v∈[ua​(x),ub​(x)].
(36)

This says exactly that

−(αuˉ(x)+γpˉ(x))∈N[ua(x),ub(x)](uˉ(x)),-\bigl(\alpha\bar u(x)+\gamma\bar p(x)\bigr) \in N_{[u_a(x),u_b(x)]}(\bar u(x)),−(αuˉ(x)+γpˉ​(x))∈N[ua​(x),ub​(x)]​(uˉ(x)),
(37)

where NCN_CNC​ denotes the normal cone to the convex set CCC. The normal-cone identity for projections gives the formula above.

Equivalently:

  • if ua<uˉ<ubu_a<\bar u<u_bua​<uˉ<ub​, then αuˉ+γpˉ=0\alpha\bar u+\gamma\bar p=0αuˉ+γpˉ​=0;

  • if uˉ=ua\bar u=u_auˉ=ua​, then αuˉ+γpˉ≥0\alpha\bar u+\gamma\bar p\ge0αuˉ+γpˉ​≥0;

  • if uˉ=ub\bar u=u_buˉ=ub​, then αuˉ+γpˉ≤0\alpha\bar u+\gamma\bar p\le0αuˉ+γpˉ​≤0.

Dirichlet Boundary Control¶

For Dirichlet boundary control, the state equation is

{−Δy+σy=fin Ω,y=uon Γ.\begin{cases} -\Delta y+\sigma y=f & \text{in }\Omega,\\ y=u & \text{on }\Gamma. \end{cases}{−Δy+σy=fy=u​in Ω,on Γ.​
(38)

Now the control cannot be an arbitrary element of L2(Γ)L^2(\Gamma)L2(Γ) if the state is required to lie in H1(Ω)H^1(\Omega)H1(Ω). The natural space is

U=H1/2(Γ).U=H^{1/2}(\Gamma).U=H1/2(Γ).
(39)

Let V0:=H01(Ω)V_0:=H_0^1(\Omega)V0​:=H01​(Ω). By the trace theorem, there exists a continuous lifting operator

R:H1/2(Γ)→H1(Ω)R:H^{1/2}(\Gamma)\to H^1(\Omega)R:H1/2(Γ)→H1(Ω)
(40)

such that

γ(Ru)=u.\gamma(Ru)=u.γ(Ru)=u.
(41)

Write

y=w+Ru,w∈H01(Ω).y=w+Ru, \qquad w\in H_0^1(\Omega).y=w+Ru,w∈H01​(Ω).
(42)

The weak equation for www is

a(w,v)=F(v)−a(Ru,v)∀v∈H01(Ω).a(w,v)=F(v)-a(Ru,v) \qquad \forall v\in H_0^1(\Omega).a(w,v)=F(v)−a(Ru,v)∀v∈H01​(Ω).
(43)

Since aaa is coercive on H01(Ω)H_0^1(\Omega)H01​(Ω), Lax-Milgram gives a unique w∈H01(Ω)w\in H_0^1(\Omega)w∈H01​(Ω), hence a unique state y=w+Ruy=w+Ruy=w+Ru.

The estimate is

∥y∥H1(Ω)≤C(∥f∥H−1(Ω)+∥u∥H1/2(Γ)).\|y\|_{H^1(\Omega)} \le C\left( \|f\|_{H^{-1}(\Omega)} + \|u\|_{H^{1/2}(\Gamma)} \right).∥y∥H1(Ω)​≤C(∥f∥H−1(Ω)​+∥u∥H1/2(Γ)​).
(44)

Thus the Dirichlet control-to-state map is continuous from H1/2(Γ)H^{1/2}(\Gamma)H1/2(Γ) into H1(Ω)H^1(\Omega)H1(Ω).

Dirichlet Gradient and Regularity Warning¶

For Dirichlet control, it is useful to use the lifted variable y=w+Ruy=w+Ruy=w+Ru, with w∈H01(Ω)w\in H_0^1(\Omega)w∈H01​(Ω). The weak constraint is

a(w,v)−F(v)+a(Ru,v)=0∀v∈H01(Ω).a(w,v)-F(v)+a(Ru,v)=0 \qquad \forall v\in H_0^1(\Omega).a(w,v)−F(v)+a(Ru,v)=0∀v∈H01​(Ω).
(45)

The Lagrangian is

L(w,u,p)=12∥w+Ru−yd∥L2(Ω)2+α2∥u∥U2−[a(w,p)−F(p)+a(Ru,p)],\mathcal L(w,u,p) = \frac12\|w+Ru-y_d\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 + \frac\alpha2\|u\|_U^2 - \bigl[ a(w,p)-F(p)+a(Ru,p) \bigr],L(w,u,p)=21​∥w+Ru−yd​∥L2(Ω)2​+2α​∥u∥U2​−[a(w,p)−F(p)+a(Ru,p)],
(46)

where p∈H01(Ω)p\in H_0^1(\Omega)p∈H01​(Ω) is the Lagrange multiplier. Stationarity with respect to ppp gives the lifted state equation. Stationarity with respect to www gives, for every η∈H01(Ω)\eta\in H_0^1(\Omega)η∈H01​(Ω),

DwL(w,u,p) η=(y−yd,η)L2(Ω)−a(η,p)=0.D_w\mathcal L(w,u,p)\,\eta = (y-y_d,\eta)_{L^2(\Omega)} - a(\eta,p) =0.Dw​L(w,u,p)η=(y−yd​,η)L2(Ω)​−a(η,p)=0.
(47)

Therefore the adjoint equation is

a(η,p)=(y−yd,η)L2(Ω)∀η∈H01(Ω).a(\eta,p)=(y-y_d,\eta)_{L^2(\Omega)} \qquad \forall \eta\in H_0^1(\Omega).a(η,p)=(y−yd​,η)L2(Ω)​∀η∈H01​(Ω).
(48)

The control derivative is obtained by differentiating the same Lagrangian with respect to uuu. For h∈U=H1/2(Γ)h\in U=H^{1/2}(\Gamma)h∈U=H1/2(Γ),

DuL(w,u,p) h=α⟨RUu,h⟩U′,U+(y−yd,Rh)L2(Ω)−a(Rh,p).D_u\mathcal L(w,u,p)\,h = \alpha\langle R_U u,h\rangle_{U',U} + (y-y_d,Rh)_{L^2(\Omega)} - a(Rh,p).Du​L(w,u,p)h=α⟨RU​u,h⟩U′,U​+(y−yd​,Rh)L2(Ω)​−a(Rh,p).
(49)

The subtle point is that RhRhRh is generally not an element of H01(Ω)H_0^1(\Omega)H01​(Ω), because its trace is hhh. Therefore one cannot simply insert RhRhRh as a test function in the adjoint equation, which is valid only for zero-trace variations.

Assume for a moment that ppp is smooth enough for Green’s identity to be used classically. Since the adjoint satisfies

−Δp+σp=y−ydin Ω,p=0on Γ,-\Delta p+\sigma p=y-y_d \qquad\text{in }\Omega, \qquad p=0 \qquad\text{on }\Gamma,−Δp+σp=y−yd​in Ω,p=0on Γ,
(50)

we compute

a(Rh,p)=∫Ω∇(Rh)⋅∇p dx+σ∫ΩRh p dx.a(Rh,p) = \int_\Omega \nabla(Rh)\cdot\nabla p\,dx + \sigma\int_\Omega Rh\,p\,dx.a(Rh,p)=∫Ω​∇(Rh)⋅∇pdx+σ∫Ω​Rhpdx.
(51)

Integrating the first term by parts gives

a(Rh,p)=∫ΩRh(−Δp+σp) dx+∫Γγ(Rh) ∂np ds.a(Rh,p) = \int_\Omega Rh(-\Delta p+\sigma p)\,dx + \int_\Gamma \gamma(Rh)\,\partial_n p\,ds.a(Rh,p)=∫Ω​Rh(−Δp+σp)dx+∫Γ​γ(Rh)∂n​pds.
(52)

Using γ(Rh)=h\gamma(Rh)=hγ(Rh)=h and the strong adjoint equation, this becomes

a(Rh,p)=(y−yd,Rh)L2(Ω)+∫Γh ∂np ds.a(Rh,p) = (y-y_d,Rh)_{L^2(\Omega)} + \int_\Gamma h\,\partial_n p\,ds.a(Rh,p)=(y−yd​,Rh)L2(Ω)​+∫Γ​h∂n​pds.
(53)

Hence the two non-regularization terms in the Lagrangian control derivative reduce to the boundary term

(y−yd,Rh)L2(Ω)−a(Rh,p)=−⟨∂np,h⟩H−1/2,H1/2.(y-y_d,Rh)_{L^2(\Omega)}-a(Rh,p) = -\langle \partial_n p,h\rangle_{H^{-1/2},H^{1/2}}.(y−yd​,Rh)L2(Ω)​−a(Rh,p)=−⟨∂n​p,h⟩H−1/2,H1/2​.
(54)

Hence the control stationarity condition is naturally written as

αRUuˉ−∂npˉ=0in H−1/2(Γ),\alpha R_U \bar u-\partial_n \bar p=0 \qquad\text{in }H^{-1/2}(\Gamma),αRU​uˉ−∂n​pˉ​=0in H−1/2(Γ),
(55)

where RU:U→U′R_U:U\to U'RU​:U→U′ is the Riesz map of H1/2(Γ)H^{1/2}(\Gamma)H1/2(Γ).

This is the key distinction from Neumann control:

  • Neumann control with u∈L2(Γ)u\in L^2(\Gamma)u∈L2(Γ) gives an L2(Γ)L^2(\Gamma)L2(Γ) gradient involving the trace γp\gamma pγp;

  • Dirichlet control gives a boundary gradient involving the normal derivative ∂np\partial_n p∂n​p, which is generally only an element of H−1/2(Γ)H^{-1/2}(\Gamma)H−1/2(Γ).

An L2(Γ)L^2(\Gamma)L2(Γ) projection formula for Dirichlet control is therefore a formal or additional-regularity statement. It is valid, for instance, if ∂np∈L2(Γ)\partial_n p\in L^2(\Gamma)∂n​p∈L2(Γ) and the control is regularized in L2(Γ)L^2(\Gamma)L2(Γ):

uˉ=P[ua,ub](1α∂npˉ).\bar u = P_{[u_a,u_b]} \left( \frac1\alpha\partial_n\bar p \right).uˉ=P[ua​,ub​]​(α1​∂n​pˉ​).
(56)

Without such regularity, the correct condition is the variational inequality in the duality pairing between H−1/2(Γ)H^{-1/2}(\Gamma)H−1/2(Γ) and H1/2(Γ)H^{1/2}(\Gamma)H1/2(Γ).

Parameter Estimation Problem¶

We now consider an inverse problem in which the unknown is a coefficient in the PDE. Let

0<qmin⁡≤q(x)≤qmax⁡<∞a.e. in Ω.0<q_{\min}\le q(x)\le q_{\max}<\infty \qquad\text{a.e. in }\Omega.0<qmin​≤q(x)≤qmax​<∞a.e. in Ω.
(57)

For each admissible coefficient qqq, the state y=y(q)∈H01(Ω)y=y(q)\in H_0^1(\Omega)y=y(q)∈H01​(Ω) is defined by

∫Ωq∇y⋅∇v dx=⟨f,v⟩H−1,H01∀v∈H01(Ω).\int_\Omega q\nabla y\cdot\nabla v\,dx = \langle f,v\rangle_{H^{-1},H_0^1} \qquad \forall v\in H_0^1(\Omega).∫Ω​q∇y⋅∇vdx=⟨f,v⟩H−1,H01​​∀v∈H01​(Ω).
(58)

In strong form this corresponds to

{−∇⋅(q∇y)=fin Ω,y=0on Γ.\begin{cases} -\nabla\cdot(q\nabla y)=f & \text{in }\Omega,\\ y=0 & \text{on }\Gamma. \end{cases}{−∇⋅(q∇y)=fy=0​in Ω,on Γ.​
(59)

The coefficient identification problem is to choose qqq so that y(q)y(q)y(q) fits observed data ydy_dyd​. A standard Tikhonov functional is

j(q)=12∥y(q)−yd∥L2(Ω)2+α2∥q−qref∥L2(Ω)2.j(q) = \frac12\|y(q)-y_d\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 + \frac\alpha2\|q-q_{\mathrm{ref}}\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2.j(q)=21​∥y(q)−yd​∥L2(Ω)2​+2α​∥q−qref​∥L2(Ω)2​.
(60)

The admissible set is often taken as

Qad={q∈L∞(Ω):qa≤q≤qb a.e. in Ω},Q_{\mathrm{ad}} = \{q\in L^\infty(\Omega): q_a\le q\le q_b \text{ a.e. in }\Omega\},Qad​={q∈L∞(Ω):qa​≤q≤qb​ a.e. in Ω},
(61)

with 0<qa≤qb0<q_a\le q_b0<qa​≤qb​ a.e.

For every q∈Qadq\in Q_{\mathrm{ad}}q∈Qad​, the bilinear form

aq(y,v):=∫Ωq∇y⋅∇v dxa_q(y,v):=\int_\Omega q\nabla y\cdot\nabla v\,dxaq​(y,v):=∫Ω​q∇y⋅∇vdx
(62)

is continuous and coercive on H01(Ω)H_0^1(\Omega)H01​(Ω):

∣aq(y,v)∣≤∥q∥L∞(Ω)∥∇y∥L2(Ω)∥∇v∥L2(Ω),|a_q(y,v)| \le \|q\|_{L^\infty(\Omega)} \|\nabla y\|_{L^2(\Omega)} \|\nabla v\|_{L^2(\Omega)},∣aq​(y,v)∣≤∥q∥L∞(Ω)​∥∇y∥L2(Ω)​∥∇v∥L2(Ω)​,
(63)
aq(v,v)≥qmin⁡∥∇v∥L2(Ω)2.a_q(v,v) \ge q_{\min}\|\nabla v\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2.aq​(v,v)≥qmin​∥∇v∥L2(Ω)2​.
(64)

Lax-Milgram therefore gives a unique state y(q)y(q)y(q) and the stability estimate

∥y(q)∥H01(Ω)≤CPqmin⁡∥f∥H−1(Ω).\|y(q)\|_{H_0^1(\Omega)} \le \frac{C_P}{q_{\min}}\|f\|_{H^{-1}(\Omega)}.∥y(q)∥H01​(Ω)​≤qmin​CP​​∥f∥H−1(Ω)​.
(65)

Sensitivity Equation for the Coefficient¶

The coefficient-to-state map is nonlinear. We derive its derivative.

Let δq∈L∞(Ω)\delta q\in L^\infty(\Omega)δq∈L∞(Ω) be a perturbation such that q+tδqq+t\delta qq+tδq remains uniformly positive for small ttt. Let

yt:=y(q+tδq),y:=y(q).y_t:=y(q+t\delta q), \qquad y:=y(q).yt​:=y(q+tδq),y:=y(q).
(66)

Subtracting the two weak state equations gives

∫Ωq∇(yt−y)⋅∇v dx=−t∫Ωδq∇yt⋅∇v dx∀v∈H01(Ω).\int_\Omega q\nabla(y_t-y)\cdot\nabla v\,dx = -t\int_\Omega \delta q\nabla y_t\cdot\nabla v\,dx \qquad \forall v\in H_0^1(\Omega).∫Ω​q∇(yt​−y)⋅∇vdx=−t∫Ω​δq∇yt​⋅∇vdx∀v∈H01​(Ω).
(67)

Divide by ttt and pass formally to the limit t→0t\to0t→0. The sensitivity

z:=y′(q)δqz:=y'(q)\delta qz:=y′(q)δq
(68)

solves

∫Ωq∇z⋅∇v dx=−∫Ωδq∇y⋅∇v dx∀v∈H01(Ω).\int_\Omega q\nabla z\cdot\nabla v\,dx = -\int_\Omega \delta q\nabla y\cdot\nabla v\,dx \qquad \forall v\in H_0^1(\Omega).∫Ω​q∇z⋅∇vdx=−∫Ω​δq∇y⋅∇vdx∀v∈H01​(Ω).
(69)

In strong form,

{−∇⋅(q∇z)=∇⋅(δq∇y)in Ω,z=0on Γ.\begin{cases} -\nabla\cdot(q\nabla z)=\nabla\cdot(\delta q\nabla y) & \text{in }\Omega,\\ z=0 & \text{on }\Gamma. \end{cases}{−∇⋅(q∇z)=∇⋅(δq∇y)z=0​in Ω,on Γ.​
(70)

The equation is well posed because the right-hand side is bounded on H01(Ω)H_0^1(\Omega)H01​(Ω):

∣∫Ωδq∇y⋅∇v dx∣≤∥δq∥L∞(Ω)∥∇y∥L2(Ω)∥∇v∥L2(Ω).\left| \int_\Omega \delta q\nabla y\cdot\nabla v\,dx \right| \le \|\delta q\|_{L^\infty(\Omega)} \|\nabla y\|_{L^2(\Omega)} \|\nabla v\|_{L^2(\Omega)}.∣∣​∫Ω​δq∇y⋅∇vdx∣∣​≤∥δq∥L∞(Ω)​∥∇y∥L2(Ω)​∥∇v∥L2(Ω)​.
(71)

Thus

∥z∥H01(Ω)≤C∥δq∥L∞(Ω)∥y∥H01(Ω).\|z\|_{H_0^1(\Omega)} \le C \|\delta q\|_{L^\infty(\Omega)} \|y\|_{H_0^1(\Omega)}.∥z∥H01​(Ω)​≤C∥δq∥L∞(Ω)​∥y∥H01​(Ω)​.
(72)

This proves that the derivative maps bounded coefficient perturbations to state perturbations continuously.

Adjoint Gradient for Parameter Estimation¶

We again derive the adjoint and the gradient through the Lagrangian. The weak state constraint is

∫Ωq∇y⋅∇v dx−⟨f,v⟩H−1,H01=0∀v∈H01(Ω).\int_\Omega q\nabla y\cdot\nabla v\,dx - \langle f,v\rangle_{H^{-1},H_0^1} =0 \qquad \forall v\in H_0^1(\Omega).∫Ω​q∇y⋅∇vdx−⟨f,v⟩H−1,H01​​=0∀v∈H01​(Ω).
(73)

Introduce

L(y,q,p)=12∥y−yd∥L2(Ω)2+α2∥q−qref∥L2(Ω)2−[∫Ωq∇y⋅∇p dx−⟨f,p⟩H−1,H01].\mathcal L(y,q,p) = \frac12\|y-y_d\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 + \frac\alpha2\|q-q_{\mathrm{ref}}\|_{L^2(\Omega)}^2 - \left[ \int_\Omega q\nabla y\cdot\nabla p\,dx - \langle f,p\rangle_{H^{-1},H_0^1} \right].L(y,q,p)=21​∥y−yd​∥L2(Ω)2​+2α​∥q−qref​∥L2(Ω)2​−[∫Ω​q∇y⋅∇pdx−⟨f,p⟩H−1,H01​​].
(74)

Stationarity with respect to ppp gives the state equation. Stationarity with respect to yyy gives, for every η∈H01(Ω)\eta\in H_0^1(\Omega)η∈H01​(Ω),

DyL(y,q,p) η=(y−yd,η)L2(Ω)−∫Ωq∇η⋅∇p dx=0.D_y\mathcal L(y,q,p)\,\eta = (y-y_d,\eta)_{L^2(\Omega)} - \int_\Omega q\nabla \eta\cdot\nabla p\,dx =0.Dy​L(y,q,p)η=(y−yd​,η)L2(Ω)​−∫Ω​q∇η⋅∇pdx=0.
(75)

Therefore

∫Ωq∇η⋅∇p dx=(y−yd,η)L2(Ω)∀η∈H01(Ω).\int_\Omega q\nabla \eta\cdot\nabla p\,dx = (y-y_d,\eta)_{L^2(\Omega)} \qquad \forall \eta\in H_0^1(\Omega).∫Ω​q∇η⋅∇pdx=(y−yd​,η)L2(Ω)​∀η∈H01​(Ω).
(76)

Equivalently, in strong form,

{−∇⋅(q∇p)=y−ydin Ω,p=0on Γ.\begin{cases} -\nabla\cdot(q\nabla p)=y-y_d & \text{in }\Omega,\\ p=0 & \text{on }\Gamma. \end{cases}{−∇⋅(q∇p)=y−yd​p=0​in Ω,on Γ.​
(77)

The coefficient derivative is

DqL(y,q,p) δq=∫Ω[α(q−qref)−∇y⋅∇p]δq dx.D_q\mathcal L(y,q,p)\,\delta q = \int_\Omega \left[ \alpha(q-q_{\mathrm{ref}}) - \nabla y\cdot\nabla p \right] \delta q\,dx.Dq​L(y,q,p)δq=∫Ω​[α(q−qref​)−∇y⋅∇p]δqdx.
(78)

Since the state and adjoint equations are precisely the stationarity conditions in ppp and yyy, this is the derivative of the reduced functional:

j′(q)δq=DqL(y,q,p) δq.j'(q)\delta q = D_q\mathcal L(y,q,p)\,\delta q.j′(q)δq=Dq​L(y,q,p)δq.
(79)

If the product ∇y⋅∇p\nabla y\cdot\nabla p∇y⋅∇p belongs to L2(Ω)L^2(\Omega)L2(Ω), then the L2(Ω)L^2(\Omega)L2(Ω)-gradient is

∇j(q)=α(q−qref)−∇y⋅∇p.\nabla j(q) = \alpha(q-q_{\mathrm{ref}}) - \nabla y\cdot\nabla p.∇j(q)=α(q−qref​)−∇y⋅∇p.
(80)

In the minimal energy setting, ∇y,∇p∈L2(Ω)d\nabla y,\nabla p\in L^2(\Omega)^d∇y,∇p∈L2(Ω)d, so the product is a priori only in L1(Ω)L^1(\Omega)L1(Ω). Therefore, the formula is always valid as a duality expression, while interpreting it as an L2L^2L2 gradient requires additional regularity or a discrete finite element setting.

Box Constraints for Parameter Estimation¶

Let qˉ∈Qad\bar q\in Q_{\mathrm{ad}}qˉ​∈Qad​ be a local minimizer, with state yˉ\bar yyˉ​ and adjoint pˉ\bar ppˉ​. The first-order condition is the variational inequality

j′(qˉ)(r−qˉ)≥0∀r∈Qad.j'(\bar q)(r-\bar q)\ge0 \qquad \forall r\in Q_{\mathrm{ad}}.j′(qˉ​)(r−qˉ​)≥0∀r∈Qad​.
(81)

Using the adjoint formula, this reads

∫Ω[α(qˉ−qref)−∇yˉ⋅∇pˉ](r−qˉ) dx≥0∀r∈Qad.\int_\Omega \left[ \alpha(\bar q-q_{\mathrm{ref}}) - \nabla\bar y\cdot\nabla\bar p \right] (r-\bar q)\,dx \ge0 \qquad \forall r\in Q_{\mathrm{ad}}.∫Ω​[α(qˉ​−qref​)−∇yˉ​⋅∇pˉ​](r−qˉ​)dx≥0∀r∈Qad​.
(82)

When the gradient is an L2(Ω)L^2(\Omega)L2(Ω) function, this is equivalent to the pointwise projection formula

qˉ=P[qa,qb](qref+1α∇yˉ⋅∇pˉ)a.e. in Ω.\bar q = P_{[q_a,q_b]} \left( q_{\mathrm{ref}} + \frac1\alpha\nabla\bar y\cdot\nabla\bar p \right) \qquad\text{a.e. in }\Omega.qˉ​=P[qa​,qb​]​(qref​+α1​∇yˉ​⋅∇pˉ​)a.e. in Ω.
(83)

The corresponding active-set interpretation is:

  • on the inactive set qa<qˉ<qbq_a<\bar q<q_bqa​<qˉ​<qb​,

    α(qˉ−qref)−∇yˉ⋅∇pˉ=0;\alpha(\bar q-q_{\mathrm{ref}}) - \nabla\bar y\cdot\nabla\bar p=0;α(qˉ​−qref​)−∇yˉ​⋅∇pˉ​=0;
    (84)

  • on the lower active set qˉ=qa\bar q=q_aqˉ​=qa​,

    α(qˉ−qref)−∇yˉ⋅∇pˉ≥0;\alpha(\bar q-q_{\mathrm{ref}}) - \nabla\bar y\cdot\nabla\bar p\ge0;α(qˉ​−qref​)−∇yˉ​⋅∇pˉ​≥0;
    (85)

  • on the upper active set qˉ=qb\bar q=q_bqˉ​=qb​,

    α(qˉ−qref)−∇yˉ⋅∇pˉ≤0.\alpha(\bar q-q_{\mathrm{ref}}) - \nabla\bar y\cdot\nabla\bar p\le0.α(qˉ​−qref​)−∇yˉ​⋅∇pˉ​≤0.
    (86)

Existence and Regularization in Inverse Problems¶

For distributed and Neumann controls, the regularization term α2∥u∥L22\frac\alpha2\|u\|_{L^2}^22α​∥u∥L22​ gives coercivity in the same Hilbert space in which the control is optimized.

For coefficient estimation, the situation is more subtle:

  • the PDE requires q∈L∞(Ω)q\in L^\infty(\Omega)q∈L∞(Ω) with a positive lower bound;

  • the Tikhonov term above controls only the L2(Ω)L^2(\Omega)L2(Ω) distance from qrefq_{\mathrm{ref}}qref​;

  • weak convergence of coefficients does not automatically imply strong convergence of the corresponding fluxes q∇yq\nabla yq∇y.

In a finite element discretization, existence is automatic because the admissible set is finite-dimensional and closed. In an infinite-dimensional analysis, one usually strengthens the compactness by choosing, for example,

α2∥q−qref∥H1(Ω)2\frac\alpha2\|q-q_{\mathrm{ref}}\|_{H^1(\Omega)}^22α​∥q−qref​∥H1(Ω)2​
(87)

or a bounded-variation regularization. These choices improve compactness and make the inverse problem stable with respect to noisy data.

This is the essential role of regularization: it is not merely a numerical penalty, but part of the mathematical formulation that turns an ill-posed inverse problem into a well-posed optimization problem.

Numerical Structure¶

Both classes of problems lead to the same computational loop:

  • solve the state equation;

  • solve the adjoint equation;

  • compute the reduced gradient;

  • apply a descent, Newton, or active-set update;

  • enforce the admissible constraints.

For Neumann boundary control, the discrete gradient contains a boundary mass matrix because the control inner product is on Γ\GammaΓ:

MΓG=αMΓU+MΓPΓ.M_\Gamma G = \alpha M_\Gamma U + M_\Gamma P_\Gamma.MΓ​G=αMΓ​U+MΓ​PΓ​.
(88)

For Dirichlet boundary control, the discrete gradient involves the discrete normal derivative of the adjoint. This is more sensitive to mesh quality and boundary approximation.

For parameter estimation, the gradient at a quadrature point has the form

gh=α(qh−qref,h)−∇yh⋅∇ph.g_h = \alpha(q_h-q_{\mathrm{ref},h}) - \nabla y_h\cdot\nabla p_h.gh​=α(qh​−qref,h​)−∇yh​⋅∇ph​.
(89)

Thus the quality of the gradient depends on the accuracy of both state and adjoint gradients. This is why coefficient identification problems often require stabilization, mesh refinement, and careful treatment of noisy data.

Summary¶

Boundary control and parameter estimation fit into the same reduced optimization framework as distributed control, but the spaces change:

  • Neumann control uses trace terms and gives gradients involving γp\gamma pγp;

  • Dirichlet control uses liftings and gives dual boundary gradients involving ∂np\partial_n p∂n​p;

  • parameter estimation differentiates the PDE operator itself and gives gradients involving ∇y⋅∇p\nabla y\cdot\nabla p∇y⋅∇p.

The main mathematical lessons are:

  • the weak formulation determines the correct control space;

  • Lax-Milgram gives the state and adjoint equations once coercivity and boundedness are checked;

  • the reduced gradient is obtained by differentiating the Lagrangian with respect to the control or parameter after imposing state and adjoint stationarity;

  • projection formulas are pointwise versions of variational inequalities;

  • inverse problems require regularization not only for algorithms, but also for stability and well-posedness.

  • Inverse problems

  • Adjoint equations

  • Regularization
